1: A type: "I waited patiently for the LORD; he turned to me and listened to my cry." The patient waiting (qāwōh qiwwîtî, the intensive form: I waited with waiting) followed by divine attention and listening is the resurrection pattern. Hebrews 5:7: "he offered up prayers and petitions with fervent cries and tears to the one who could save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverent submission." The patient waiting of Psalms 40:1 and the divine turning-to-listen is the Gethsemane-to-resurrection movement: Jesus waited through the cross; the Father turned and listened in the resurrection.
2: A type: "He lifted me out of the pit of destruction, from the muddy bog; he set my feet on a rock and made my steps firm." The rescue from the pit (bôr šĕʾôn, pit of destruction — a Sheol term) and from the mud onto a rock is resurrection imagery. Acts 2:24: "God raised him from the dead, freeing him from the agony of death, because it was impossible for death to keep its hold on him." The "pit of destruction" = Sheol = death; the "rock" with firm steps = the resurrection ground on which Christ now stands. The mud-and-pit rescue of Psalms 40:2 is the type whose fulfillment is the first Easter morning.
3: A theme: "He put a new song in my mouth, praise to our God; many will see and be filled with awe and will put their trust in the LORD." The new song (šîr ḥādāš) placed by YHWH in the rescued person's mouth — and the public witness that produces awe and trust — is the pattern of the resurrection witness. Acts 2:47: "praising God and enjoying the favor of all the people. And the Lord added to their number daily those who were being saved." The "many will see... and will put their trust in the LORD" of Psalms 40:3 describes the apostolic proclamation: the new song of resurrection praise produces faith in those who hear it.
4: A revelation of God: "Blessed is the one who makes the LORD his trust and does not turn to the proud or to those who follow falsehood." The beatitude of trust in YHWH — exclusive, not divided with human power structures or false gods — is the wisdom foundation of faith. Romans 10:11, quoting Isaiah 28:16: "Anyone who trusts in him will never be put to shame." Christ is both the object of this trust and the demonstration of its reward: the one who trusted the Father absolutely through the cross received the vindication of the resurrection (Philippians 2:9). Trust in Christ is the NT form of making "the LORD his trust."
5: A revelation of God: "You have multiplied your wonderful deeds and your thoughts toward us, O LORD my God; no one can compare to you." The divine incomparability — the accumulation of wondrous acts beyond counting — is the hymnic foundation of Psalm 40. In Christ, the incomparable wondrous deed is the cross and resurrection: 1 Corinthians 1:18, "the message of the cross is foolishness to those who are perishing, but to us who are being saved it is the power of God." The "wonderful deeds" of the Exodus and the Psalter are multiplied and surpassed in Christ — the deed that could not be counted because it is inexhaustible in its effects.
6: A direct citation: "Sacrifice and grain offering you did not want; but you have given me opened ears; burnt offering and sin offering you have not asked for." Hebrews 10:5-7 quotes the LXX form of vv6-8 — "a body you prepared for me" (where MT has "opened ears") — as the very words of Christ entering the world: "Sacrifice and offering you did not desire, but a body you prepared for me; with burnt offerings and sin offerings you were not pleased. Then I said, 'Here I am — it is written about me in the scroll — I have come to do your will, my God.'" Psalms 40:6 is the theological fulcrum of Hebrews 10's argument that the entire Levitical sacrifice system is superseded by Christ's one offering of his body.
7: A direct citation: "Then I said, 'Here I am; in the scroll of the book it is written about me.'" This verse is the centerpiece of Hebrews 10's Christological reading: the whole OT scroll was written about Christ, and he comes to fulfill it. Luke 24:44: "Everything must be fulfilled that is written about me in the Law of Moses, the Prophets and the Psalms." John 5:39: "These are the very Scriptures that testify about me." The "Here I am" (hinnēnî) is the classic prophetic availability formula (Isaiah 6:8: "Here am I. Send me!") — Christ entering history as the one who fulfills the whole written anticipation of the OT.
8: A direct citation: "I delight to do your will, O my God; your instruction is within my heart." Hebrews 10:7 quotes this as Christ's words. John 6:38: "For I have come down from heaven not to do my will but to do the will of him who sent me." John 4:34: "My food is to do the will of him who sent me." Philippians 2:8: "he humbled himself by becoming obedient to death — even death on a cross." The "delight to do your will" of Psalms 40:8 is not mere compliance but joyful, interior alignment — and the law "within the heart" is the New Covenant promise of Jeremiah 31:33, embodied in Christ before being extended to his people through the Spirit.
9: A type: "I have announced righteousness in the great assembly; I have not held my lips back — LORD, you know it." The public proclamation of righteousness in the community's assembly — open, unrestrained — is the prophetic-priestly role of the Davidic king. Christ's public teaching in the temple courts (Luke 19:47; Psalms 20:1) and his bold proclamation of the gospel is the fulfillment: John 18:20, "I have spoken openly to the world. I always taught in synagogues or at the temple, where all the Jews come together. I said nothing in secret." The "great assembly" proclamation of Psalms 40:9 is the type of Christ's public Messianic declaration.
10: A type: "I have not hidden your righteousness in my heart; I have declared your faithfulness and your salvation; I have not concealed your steadfast love or your truth from the great assembly." The foursome of what has been declared — righteousness, faithfulness, salvation, steadfast love, truth — is the covenant-attribute catalogue. Titus 3:4-5: "But when the kindness and love of God our Savior appeared... he saved us." Christ's entire ministry is the unconcealedproclamation of the divine attributes of Psalms 40:10 — each one made visible in his person and work: righteousness (2 Corinthians 5:21), faithfulness (Hebrews 10:23), salvation (Luke 4:18), steadfast love (John 15:13), truth (John 14:6).
11: A revelation of God: "As for you, LORD, do not withhold your compassion from me; let your steadfast love and truth continually protect me." The prayer for the divine compassion and ḥesed-truth pair to be protection is the Psalm's personal petition after the public proclamation of vv9-10. John 1:14 describes Christ as "full of grace and truth" — the ḥesed-ʾĕmet (steadfast love and truth) of Psalms 40:11 is the divine pair that is embodied in the Word made flesh. The prayer for ḥesed-ʾĕmet as protection is answered when "grace and truth came through Jesus Christ" (John 1:17).
12: A type: "For countless evils have closed in around me; my iniquities have caught up with me until I cannot see clearly — they are more than the hairs of my head, and my courage fails." The return from the praise of vv1-11 to the crisis of personal sin — iniquities that blind and overwhelm — is the pastoral honesty of the Psalm. For Christ, who bears not his own iniquities but ours: Isaiah 53:6, "the LORD has laid on him the iniquity of us all." The "countless evils" and "iniquities more than hairs of my head" that overwhelm the Psalmist become, at Gethsemane and the cross, the full weight of human sin that Christ takes on voluntarily.
13: A type: "Please, LORD, come to rescue me; LORD, hurry to help me." The urgent cry for divine rescue — speed, urgency — is the Psalm's Gethsemane moment: the human vulnerability of the one who genuinely needs help. Hebrews 5:7: "he offered up prayers and petitions with fervent cries and tears." The urgency of "hurry to help me" is the cry of the incarnate Son in the Passion — not self-sufficient, not performing strength, but genuinely needing the Father's rescue. The Father's answer is the resurrection: the rescue comes, but through death, not avoiding it.
14: A shadow: "Let those who seek to take my life be put to shame and confounded; let those who wish me harm be turned back in disgrace." The imprecatory prayer against those seeking the Psalmist's life — shame and disgrace on the persecutors — is the Psalm's justice element. At the resurrection, the scheme of those who sought Christ's death is definitively "put to shame": 1 Corinthians 2:8, "None of the rulers of this age understood it, for if they had, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory." The cross appeared as their victory; the resurrection revealed it as their shame. The full disgrace awaits the final judgment (Revelation 20:11-15).
15: A shadow: "Let those who mock me with 'Aha! Aha!' be horrified by their own shame." The "Aha! Aha!" mockers — those who take pleasure in the Psalmist's distress — are the Passion crowd. Matthew 27:39-40: "Those who passed by hurled insults at him, shaking their heads... 'Come down from the cross, if you are the Son of God!'" Mark 15:29 uses the same mocking gesture. The "Aha!" gloating of Psalms 40:15 is enacted at the crucifixion — and the promised reversal (their horror and shame) is the eschatological judgment that the resurrection initiates.
16: A revelation of God: "Let all who seek you rejoice and be glad in you; let those who love your salvation always say, 'Great is the LORD!'" The communal rejoicing of the seekers — gladness in YHWH, constant praise of his salvation — is the eschatological community that the Psalm envisions as the answer to the Psalmist's crisis. Revelation 19:7: "Let us rejoice and be glad and give him glory! For the wedding of the Lamb has come." The "great is the LORD" of Psalms 40:16 becomes the great chorus of Revelation's heavenly worship — those who love his salvation praising the risen Christ throughout eternity.
17: A direct connection: "As for me, I am poor and needy; yet the Lord thinks of me. You are my help and my deliverer — do not be slow, my God." The closing declaration — "poor and needy" (ʿānî wĕʾebyôn) yet beloved and thought of by YHWH — is the Psalm's final Christological statement. 2 Corinthians 8:9: "For you know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ, that though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you through his poverty might become rich." Christ voluntarily entered the "poor and needy" condition of Psalms 40:17 — the incarnation is the divine Lord becoming the one who needs the Father's help and deliverance. And the Father is "not slow": the resurrection is the timely answer.
